skill.name: routeros-netinstall
skill.description: MikroTik netinstall-cli for automated RouterOS device flashing. Use when: automating netinstall, writing scripts that invoke netinstall-cli, building netinstall tooling, understanding etherboot/BOOTP/TFTP protocols, working with RouterOS package files (.npk), using modescript or configure script, or when the user mentions netinstall, etherboot, or device flashing.
skill.summary: MikroTik RouterOS netinstall automation guide with command syntax, package management, and scripting documentation.
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: This skill is a documentation guide for MikroTik RouterOS netinstall-cli. Static analysis flagged 107 potential issues, but evaluation shows all are false positives. The skill documents a legitimate network administration tool with external command references being markdown documentation syntax, not executable code. Hardcoded URLs are legitimate MikroTik download endpoints. No prompt injection or malicious patterns detected.

high_findings.title: Hardcoded MikroTik Download URLs
high_findings.description: The skill contains hardcoded URLs to download.mikrotik.com for RouterOS packages and netinstall-cli binaries. These are legitimate vendor endpoints used for network administration.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs point to official MikroTik CDN which is verified safe. No data exfiltration or credential harvesting patterns present.

medium_findings.title: Markdown Code Fenced Shell Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill contains shell command examples using backtick syntax and fenced code blocks. These are documentation examples for netinstall-cli usage, not executable code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All shell commands are markdown documentation examples showing netinstall-cli syntax. No dynamic code execution or user input sanitization issues.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Network Configuration Examples
low_findings.description: The skill documents network configuration examples including IP addresses (192.168.88.2/24) for netinstall server setup.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Standard documentation of network configuration patterns. No sensitive data exposure or malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Etherboot Entry Methods Documentation
low_findings.description: The skill documents device recovery methods including reset button, serial console, and RouterOS settings for entering etherboot mode.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Legitimate technical documentation for device recovery procedures. No reconnaissance or vulnerability exploitation patterns.

content.value_statement: Installing RouterOS on multiple MikroTik devices manually is time-consuming and error-prone. This skill provides Claude Code with comprehensive netinstall-cli documentation for automated, repeatable firmware installation.
actual_capabilities: Generate netinstall-cli command syntax with proper flag combinations
Document RouterOS package naming conventions and download URLs
Explain configure script vs mode script execution order
Provide examples for empty config, keep config, and first-boot mode scenarios
Clarify etherboot requirements and device entry methods
Detail architecture-specific package naming for ARM, ARM64, MIPS variants
limitations: Does not execute netinstall-cli commands directly
Cannot verify device etherboot status or network connectivity
Does not download RouterOS packages or binaries
Cannot trigger device reset or manage hardware physically

use_cases.title: Automated RouterOS Deployment Scripts
use_cases.description: Network administrators can use this skill to generate shell scripts that automate netinstall-cli execution across multiple MikroTik devices. The skill provides proper command syntax, flag combinations, and package ordering rules.

use_cases.title: Troubleshooting RouterOS Installation Failures
use_cases.description: System engineers can reference this skill to understand etherboot entry methods, network configuration requirements, and common failure causes like DHCP snooping or USB adapter compatibility issues.

use_cases.title: Building Custom RouterOS Provisioning Pipelines
use_cases.description: DevOps teams can integrate this documentation to build automated provisioning pipelines that handle package downloads, architecture detection, and first-boot configuration via mode scripts.

prompt_templates.title: Basic netinstall Command Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a netinstall-cli command to install RouterOS 7.22 on an ARM64 device with empty configuration. Use interface eth0 and include the system package first.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Script for First-Boot Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a configure script that sets up basic router configuration with the default admin password and enables container mode. Include timing considerations.

prompt_templates.title: Mode Script for Device Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a mode script that configures device-mode to advanced with container support. Explain the execution order relative to configure scripts.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Architecture Package Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the package naming convention differences between x86 and ARM architectures. Include example filenames for all supported RouterOS 7 architectures.

output_examples.input: How do I keep the existing router configuration while reinstalling RouterOS?
output_examples.output: To preserve the configuration database, run netinstall-cli WITHOUT the -r or -e flags. The tool will: 1) Download existing configuration from the device, 2) Reformat the system drive, 3) Upload the configuration back. Important: This does not preserve user files like Dude or User Manager databases. For complete preservation, backup these separately before reinstall.

output_examples.input: What is the correct order of packages in netinstall-cli?
output_examples.output: Always list the system package (routeros-X.XX-arch.npk) FIRST in the package list. Netinstall uses this to detect device architecture. Additional packages like container, dhcp, or wireless can follow in any order. Multi-arch package sets are allowed; netinstall will use only packages matching the detected architecture.

output_examples.input: Explain the difference between mode script and configure script execution.
output_examples.output: Mode script (-sm) runs FIRST on first boot, before any other configuration. Configure script (-s) runs AFTER mode script as part of the default-configuration stage. If mode script changes device-mode, the device reboots immediately after mode script completes. Use mode script for first-boot state that must happen before configuration, like /system/device-mode updates.

best_practices: Always put the system package (routeros-*.npk) first in the package list to enable architecture detection
Use static IP configuration on the netinstall host to avoid DHCP conflicts during installation
Document your configure scripts and store them in version control for repeatable deployments
anti_patterns: Do not run netinstall without understanding which flags affect configuration preservation
Do not skip verifying device etherboot status before starting installation
Do not mix architecture-specific packages from different RouterOS versions

faq.question: What ports does netinstall-cli use?
faq.answer: Netinstall uses BOOTP/DHCP ports for discovery and TFTP for image transfer. It requires root privileges for these privileged network operations. Avoid running other DHCP servers on the same network segment during installation.

faq.question: Does netinstall erase the RouterOS license key?
faq.answer: No. Netinstall reformats the system drive but does NOT erase the RouterOS license key or reset RouterBOOT settings. The license remains active after reinstallation.

faq.question: Can I reinstall multiple devices in one session?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the -m flag to enable repeated installs. Add -o to only reinstall a given MAC address once per run. Multiple netinstall instances require the -c flag.

faq.question: What happens if I do not use -r or -e?
faq.answer: Without these flags, netinstall preserves the existing configuration database. It downloads the config, reformats the device, and uploads the config back. User files and databases (Dude, User Manager) are NOT preserved.

faq.question: How do I run netinstall-cli on non-x86 hosts?
faq.answer: On x86_64 Linux, run directly. On ARM/ARM64 Linux, use QEMU user-mode (qemu-i386-static). On macOS, run Linux in a VM with bridged networking. The tikoci/netinstall wrapper automates these scenarios.

faq.question: What is the timeout for configure and mode scripts?
faq.answer: Both configure script (-s) and mode script (-sm) have a 120-second timeout. MikroTik docs recommend introducing a delay before configure script execution to ensure the system is fully initialized.
